title: Tricks and Treats

jokes.text: The elaborate list of exclusions before the bet: no bathroom, no garage, no cleansers, no detergent, no furniture polish, no cut-up bits of sponge
jokes.setup: A kid agrees to drink anything for $10 — a classic gross-out dare setup
jokes.punchline: The increasingly specific list of disqualifiers (especially 'no cut-up bits of sponge') reveals that whoever is making the rules has clearly thought WAY too hard about this, with 'no cut-up bits of sponge' being an absurdly specific phobia
jokes.explanation: The escalating specificity of the exclusions is well-structured — each item is funnier than the last, with 'no cut-up bits of sponge' landing as a genuinely absurd peak. The character voice is clear even without knowing who's speaking. Functional cringe-escalation comedy.

jokes.text: 'I'm just trying to win 10 bucks here. I don't wanna die.'
jokes.setup: Bill is listing all the safety exclusions for the dare
jokes.punchline: The understatement — framing not dying as a modest, secondary concern behind winning $10 — reveals his matter-of-fact relationship with his own mortality
jokes.explanation: Beautiful economy — the $10 and 'don't wanna die' as equal-weight considerations is very Bill. The deadpan delivery implied by the flat structure makes this land as a genuine character moment. Above average for the episode.

jokes.text: 'I don't want to mess up my hair.' / 'And now step into the soundproof booth.'
jokes.setup: Bill is about to put on a blindfold for the gross-drink dare
jokes.punchline: His concern about messing up his hair is absurd vanity given the context; then 'the soundproof booth' — almost certainly a cardboard box or similarly pathetic approximation — is announced with ridiculous solemnity
jokes.explanation: Both gags work in tandem. The hair vanity is a quick character beat, but 'soundproof booth' is the real joke — the confidence with which they name their DIY contraption is classic geek-logic comedy. Rewatch bonus because we'd see what the 'booth' actually is.

jokes.text: 'AND NOW STEP INTO THE SOUNDPROOF BOOTH.' — the 'soundproof booth' is clearly just the kitchen corner with no acoustic properties whatsoever
jokes.setup: Neal grandly announces a 'soundproof booth' as if this is a sophisticated experiment
jokes.punchline: It's self-evidently not soundproof — Bill immediately hears everything — making the formal announcement absurd
jokes.explanation: The joke lands on the immediate contradiction: the 'booth' fails the moment Bill says 'I can hear everything.' Functional setup/payoff, but not especially original. Works better as a visual gag (the audience sees whatever sad corner they've designated) than as pure dialogue.

jokes.text: 'Put in some mustard. It's an ipecac. It'll make him barf.' / 'Don't make him barf. My mom cooks dinner in here!'
jokes.setup: They're plotting what to put in the mystery drink to torture Bill
jokes.punchline: Sam's objection has nothing to do with protecting Bill — it's about his mom's kitchen. The priority inversion is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The subverted concern lands well. Sam's practical domesticity versus Neal's gross ingenuity is a clean character contrast. The parental property anxiety is very true to the age.

jokes.text: 'If we're not trying to make him barf, then why are we doing this?' — said while Bill is humming three feet away
jokes.setup: Sam forbids making Bill barf; they continue conspiring
jokes.punchline: Neal articulates the absurdity of their situation — the entire premise of the dare collapses without the barf payoff — but only after they've already committed to it
jokes.explanation: Neal's self-aware observation is funny because it's correct — they have no endgame now. The existential crisis over a dare is proportionate and relatable.

jokes.text: 'I can hear everything you guys are saying.' / 'Come on, Bill! Quit listening!' / 'I can't help it.'
jokes.setup: The boys are whispering their conspiracy in front of Bill in the 'soundproof booth'
jokes.punchline: Bill politely informs them the booth doesn't work; being told to 'quit listening' as if it's a choice he's making; Bill's helpless 'I can't help it'
jokes.explanation: Three-part escalation that builds beautifully: the revelation, the absurd command, the resigned compliance. 'Quit listening' as an accusation is wonderfully illogical. Bill's 'I can't help it' is the ideal button — passive, sweet, and slightly baffled. Strong character comedy.

jokes.text: 'Then hum so you can't hear us.'
jokes.setup: Bill can hear all their scheming through the non-soundproof booth
jokes.punchline: The proposed solution — Bill humming to block himself from hearing — is the logical equivalent of putting your fingers in your own ears while someone next to you schemes, and everyone accepts this as reasonable
jokes.explanation: The logic is perfectly broken — they can't make the booth soundproof so they require the victim to self-deafen. And Bill complies. The absurdist internal consistency is very well constructed.

jokes.text: The gross cocktail assembly — mustard, cayenne pepper, pickle juice, sardines, vinegar, soy sauce, chili, jelly, dairy creamer, after-dinner mints — built while Bill hums obliviously
jokes.setup: The boys mix an increasingly disgusting concoction while Bill hums
jokes.punchline: Each ingredient is more absurd than the last; 'chili — for texture' and the incongruous after-dinner mints as a finishing touch are the twin peaks; the humming makes each reveal land harder
jokes.explanation: The structure of this sequence is its greatest asset — the rhythm of ingredient reveals against Bill's humming is almost musical. 'For texture' as justification for adding chili is the standout line. The after-dinner mints as a civilized cap to something disgusting is a good absurdist button.

jokes.text: 'Oh, it's bubbling!'
jokes.setup: The concoction is mixed together
jokes.punchline: The scientific horror of their creation bubbling is both disgusting and funny — they've accidentally made something dangerous
jokes.explanation: Functional escalation beat. The bubbling is a natural consequence of mixing acidic and basic ingredients (vinegar + dairy) but played as pure horror. Serviceable.

jokes.text: 'Let me see the money first.'
jokes.setup: They unveil the horrifying drink to Bill, who removes his blindfold
jokes.punchline: Bill's first instinct upon seeing the nightmare cocktail is not revulsion but professional prudence — make sure the money's real before proceeding
jokes.explanation: This is a great Bill moment — it captures his peculiar combination of commitment, practicality, and mild absurdism. He has accepted that he's going to drink this thing; now he just needs fair compensation. The business-like tone against the gross context is perfect. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'It's not bad.' — said after an enormous reaction to drinking the disgusting concoction
jokes.setup: Bill has just drunk the repulsive cocktail and had a huge disgusted reaction
jokes.punchline: His summary verdict is stoic understatement; then immediately recants
jokes.explanation: Bill's deadpan dignity in the face of genuine disgust is his superpower as a comic character. 'It's not bad' after an obviously terrible experience is a pure Bill line — he will not give anyone the satisfaction of an honest reaction, even briefly.

jokes.text: Harold's immediate cynical dissection of the 'free pumpkin' promotion: 'There's nothing nice about it. They give you a 20-cent pumpkin, you give them 10 grand to sell the house. Some deal.'
jokes.setup: Jean cheerfully notes that Maplefarm Realty gave the neighborhood free pumpkins
jokes.punchline: Harold instantly and accurately calculates the ROI on the pumpkins, refusing to let the gesture stand as generous
jokes.explanation: Harold's curmudgeon-dad character is perfectly deployed here. The specific math (20 cents vs. $10,000) grounds the absurdity in real-world logic. 'Some deal' as the sardonic button is very well calibrated. This could only be Harold.

jokes.text: 'Hey, that's a dirty movie.' / 'Dad, it's a Get Smart movie.'
jokes.setup: Sam mentions he's going to see 'The Nude Bomb' at the Parkway with Bill
jokes.punchline: Harold immediately assumes it's pornography; Sam has to explain it's the Get Smart movie
jokes.explanation: Good period detail — 'The Nude Bomb' (1980) was indeed the Get Smart movie, and the title legitimately sounds like an adult film. Harold's snap judgment is perfectly in character. The joke works on period-specific knowledge.

jokes.text: Harold's rant: 'He can miss a night of walking around the neighborhood beggin' like a tramp.'
jokes.setup: Mom protests Sam skipping trick-or-treating
jokes.punchline: Harold reframes trick-or-treating as vagrancy — 'beggin' like a tramp' — with complete conviction
jokes.explanation: Harold's worldview — that all traditional childhood activities are actually forms of weakness or dependency — is at its sharpest here. 'Beggin' like a tramp' is a memorable and perfectly Harold formulation. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Daniel wearing a hat as a 'costume' on Devil's Day; Lindsay calls it out: 'It kinda sucks.'
jokes.setup: Daniel shows up at the bus stop with just a hat on
jokes.punchline: His minimal-effort 'costume' versus his cool-guy persona; Lindsay's flat assessment is immediately accurate
jokes.explanation: Small moment that characterizes both Daniel (minimal effort, maximum attitude) and Lindsay (she's observant enough to call him out). The directness is refreshing.

jokes.text: The Devil's Day hair-removal foam rumor: Daniel claims seniors spray freshmen with Nair; then immediately admits 'No. That stupid rumor's been around for years.' then 'Yeah, I know. I was just goofin' on 'em.' / 'No, you weren't!'
jokes.setup: Daniel explains Devil's Day with convincing authority
jokes.punchline: He debunks his own story immediately; then claims he was faking all along; Lindsay calls his bluff; the exchange reveals he was probably scared himself
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's 'No, you weren't!' is a great punchline because it strips away Daniel's cool-guy facade. The three-beat structure (assertion, retraction, false claim of irony) is tight and reveals character. Daniel trying to save face after being caught believing his own nonsense is very teenage.

jokes.text: Millie eating Lick-a-Maid at 7:30 AM: 'It makes my spit taste like fruit juice.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay notes it's 7:30 in the morning — too early for candy
jokes.punchline: Millie's justification for early-morning candy consumption is this deeply specific and oddly personal detail
jokes.explanation: This is the kind of line Freaks and Geeks does better than almost any show — a perfectly observed, oddly specific, deeply innocent detail that's simultaneously funny and tender. 'It makes my spit taste like fruit juice' is a line you can only say with complete sincerity, and Millie's character demands sincerity. Easily quotable. Among the best character lines in the episode.

jokes.text: 'My dad says you need a muffler. Once he even threw a rock at you when you drove by.' ... 'But I guess you didn't hear it.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay tells Daniel his car is really loud
jokes.punchline: The rock-throwing anecdote is funny on its own (dad actually threw a rock at a car), but the button — 'I guess you didn't hear it' — perfectly completes the loop on the noise complaint
jokes.explanation: Beautiful two-part structure. The first revelation (dad threw a rock) is already funny, but 'I guess you didn't hear it' is a perfect button that closes the joke with elegant logic. The pause before the button — which Lindsay delivers so casually it almost lands as accidental — is what makes it exceptional. Very well constructed.

jokes.text: Daniel's delayed and alarmed reaction: 'He threw a rock at me?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just revealed her dad threw a rock at Daniel's car
jokes.punchline: Daniel's priorities — he glosses past the muffler comment but the rock is genuinely alarming information
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat. His shock is proportionate and humanizing — the cool guy is genuinely startled by the rock detail. Extends the previous joke well.

jokes.text: Daniel: 'HEY, KID, WHAT THE HELL ARE YOU EATING? IS THAT LAUNDRY SOAP?' (to Millie eating Lick-a-Maid)
jokes.setup: Millie is eating Lick-a-Maid powder at the bus stop
jokes.punchline: To an outside observer (Daniel), it genuinely looks like she's eating laundry detergent — the product's appearance is indistinguishable from cleaning products
jokes.explanation: A mild visual gag that plays on the earlier cold-open joke about not eating laundry soap. The callback makes it slightly more satisfying. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Kim calling Millie's Lick-a-Maid 'laundry soap' and Millie offering her some
jokes.setup: Kim looks at the white powder and mistakes it for detergent
jokes.punchline: Millie, unbothered, offers Kim some — as if this misidentification is just a misunderstanding of a perfectly normal snack
jokes.explanation: Extends the Millie-Lick-a-Maid gag well. Millie's cheerful offer after being insulted is perfectly in character. Kim's disgust contrasts nicely with Millie's innocence.

jokes.text: 'Thanks for the candy, Skinny.'
jokes.setup: Kim takes some of Millie's Lick-a-Maid despite passing on it
jokes.punchline: Kim takes the candy she just scorned, gives a backhanded nickname, and roars off — a complete character capsule in four words
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy. 'Thanks for the candy, Skinny' — Kim's casual cruelty disguised as friendliness, delivered while eating the candy she pretended to refuse — is perfectly Kim. The nickname lands as affectionately hostile.

jokes.text: Sam watching the car leave: 'See? My dad's right. He needs a muffler.'
jokes.setup: Daniel's car roars away loudly
jokes.punchline: Sam echoes his father's observation with complete deadpan sincerity, indifferent to the social dynamics he's just witnessed
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that also characterizes Sam — he's absorbed his father's practicality completely, immune to the cool-kid aura of Daniel's loud car. The deadpan is clean and the callback is earned.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso's hall greeting: 'If it isn't the Amelia Earhart of McKinley High.' / 'What does that mean?' / 'Well, you head to class, but you never seem to get there.'
jokes.setup: The guidance counselor spots Lindsay in the hallway
jokes.punchline: The Earhart comparison is clever — an explorer famous for disappearing en route to her destination — and Rosso delivers it with proud self-satisfaction
jokes.explanation: Rosso's joke lands because it's actually a good joke — the Earhart metaphor is specific, historical, and precisely accurate. The self-congratulatory delivery from a character who thinks he's hip makes it funnier. One of the better wordplay moments in the episode.

jokes.text: 'Don't turn me into the man here.' / '...See? It's workin' already.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay protests being humiliated; Rosso deflects by framing himself as anti-establishment
jokes.punchline: Lindsay walks away irritated; Rosso interprets her departure as evidence his method is working
jokes.explanation: Rosso's unshakeable confidence in his own ineffectiveness is his defining comedic trait. 'See? It's workin' already' after Lindsay walks away annoyed is a perfect encapsulation — he's genuinely immune to feedback. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: The English teacher's scathing book report list: 'Al Jaffee's Snappy Answers to Stupid Questions,' 'The Novelization of Star Wars,' 'Yes I Can — The Autobiography of Sammy Davis Jr.'
jokes.setup: Teacher announces she's embarrassed by the books students chose for reports
jokes.punchline: Each title revealed is funnier than the last — a Mad Magazine book, a movie tie-in novelization, and an autobiography that while legitimate is clearly not what she had in mind — a perfect 1980 geek-culture reading list
jokes.explanation: Beautifully curated period detail — every book is perfectly 1980, perfectly the kind of thing a 14-year-old boy would think counts as a book report subject. The escalation (MAD magazine book → movie novelization → Sammy Davis Jr.) is well-ordered. The Sammy Davis Jr. autobiography is the funniest pick because it's actually a real book that a kid could earnestly defend. Earned comedy through specificity.

jokes.text: 'Why don't you make out with my butt, and we'll call it love?'
jokes.setup: Someone suggests a 'foursome' as a taunt; Kim responds
jokes.punchline: Kim's comeback escalates the vulgarity in an unexpected direction that's oddly specific and completely Kim
jokes.explanation: Kim's retort is inventively structured — the parallel construction ('make out with my butt / we'll call it love') is unexpectedly elegant for something so crude. It out-grosses the initial taunt while adding a romantic-parody twist. Very Kim.

jokes.text: 'Yeah. I know. I was just messing with you.' / 'She got me with that same joke last week.' / 'It's kinda funny, though.' / 'Hilarious.'
jokes.setup: Kim has just terrified Lindsay with the fake narc story about Brian Stoker going to prison
jokes.punchline: Nick's genuine admission that the joke was funny; Lindsay's flat 'Hilarious' — a perfect deadpan from someone who was just genuinely frightened
jokes.explanation: The beat where Nick sincerely says 'It's kinda funny, though' while Lindsay is still rattled, followed by her flat 'Hilarious' is a nice character moment. The gang's casual cruelty toward newcomers is well-captured.

jokes.text: 'No, everybody hates her mother.' — Kim's confident sociological claim
jokes.setup: Daniel says Lindsay shouldn't feel dorky about handing out candy with her mom; Kim says everyone hates their mother
jokes.punchline: Kim delivers this as universal truth, with complete conviction — it's a window into her own home life delivered as comedy
jokes.explanation: Kim projecting her own family dysfunction onto all of humanity with total authority is one of her best character traits. The delivery matters enormously — this must be said with zero irony. The comedy comes from the gap between her certainty and the reality that Lindsay actually likes her mom.

jokes.text: 'If that's what you need to call it in your diary, Princess.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay nervously asks if the group outing is 'like a double date'
jokes.punchline: Daniel deflects the romantic framing with casual condescension while technically confirming it
jokes.explanation: Daniel's 'diary, Princess' dismissal is perfectly calibrated cool-guy deflection. He neither confirms nor denies, but the 'in your diary' framing makes the confirmation more intimate than a simple yes. Good economy.

jokes.text: The Cliff's Notes exchange: 'Uh, perhaps you've never heard of a little thing called... Cliff Notes?' / 'You still have to read them.'
jokes.setup: The boys lament having to read Crime and Punishment with tiny print
jokes.punchline: Neal presents Cliff's Notes as his genius solution; Sam immediately deflates it
jokes.explanation: Classic setup — Neal's 'perhaps you've never heard of' buildup makes the Cliff's Notes reveal funnier than it deserves to be. Sam's immediate deadpan correction is solid. Functional school-comedy material.

jokes.text: 'We're not adults. We're kids until we turn 18.' / 'Maybe you are. But when I hit 13, I became a man. That's only in your temple, Neal, not in the real world.'
jokes.setup: They debate whether they're adults yet in context of reading Crime and Punishment
jokes.punchline: Neal invokes his bar mitzvah as real-world manhood certification; Bill correctly points out the distinction between religious and civil adulthood
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely funny exchange with real wit — Bill's correction 'that's only in your temple, Neal, not in the real world' is a sharp line that could only come from these specific characters in this specific dynamic. Neal's self-seriousness about his bar mitzvah versus Bill's deflating precision is great. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'THE HOTDOG ON A STICK GIRLS FROM THE MALL ARE WORKING IT. THE TALL ONES?' / 'YEAH. JUDY.' / 'THEY'RE HOT. TOOTIE, TOOTIE!'
jokes.setup: Neal is explaining why he wants to go to the JC haunted house instead of trick-or-treating
jokes.punchline: 'Tootie, tootie' — Neal's personal exclamation of appreciation that is simultaneously enthusiastic and completely meaningless
jokes.explanation: 'Tootie, tootie' is an odd, funny expression of enthusiasm that's distinctly a 14-year-old trying to sound worldly. The specificity of 'the tall ones' and 'Judy' (he knows her name!) adds to the comedy of Neal's dedicated romantic research.

jokes.text: Sam's reluctant Groucho impression: 'Well... [as Groucho] That's the most ridiculous thing I've ever hoird!' / 'Groucho sucks.'
jokes.setup: Sam is being peer-pressured into trick-or-treating; Neal asks if he'll dress as Groucho again
jokes.punchline: Sam does a brief Groucho voice to show he still has it; Bill's flat 'Groucho sucks' is both a joke and a social veto
jokes.explanation: Sam's Groucho voice being shut down immediately by Bill is a good character beat. 'Groucho sucks' as an aesthetic judgment from Bill is pure Bill — flat, certain, slightly baffling in its conviction.

jokes.text: Millie's secret boyfriend reveal: 'Tommy says that if you do [French kiss] before you've been going out for 6 months, you go to hell.'
jokes.setup: Millie reveals she has a secret boyfriend from church camp
jokes.punchline: Tommy's specific theological interpretation of French kissing — it doesn't just violate values, it sends you to hell, but only if you do it before the 6-month mark
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The specificity of 'before 6 months' is what makes it exceptional — it's not a blanket prohibition, it's a loophole-adjacent theology that somehow makes French kissing permissible if you wait long enough. Only Millie could deliver this with complete sincerity. The absurdist logic feels genuinely produced by someone who exists in Millie's world. Strong enough to justify an 8+ score — it's original, character-specific, well-structured, and deeply quotable.

jokes.text: 'I DIDN'T WANT TO MAKE YOU FEEL BAD... SINCE YOU DON'T HAVE A BOYFRIEND AND ALL.' (Millie to Lindsay)
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks why Millie didn't tell her about Tommy
jokes.punchline: Millie's explanation is perfectly innocent and perfectly devastating: she was protecting Lindsay from the pain of her singleness — something Lindsay hadn't considered
jokes.explanation: Classic Millie: the kindest possible intentions producing the most inadvertently cutting remark. The cringe comes from Millie's complete inability to see how this reads. It's also a small character revelation — Lindsay's social status is lower than she thinks, and Millie, of all people, knows it. High character integration.

jokes.text: 'I didn't want to make you feel bad... since you don't have a boyfriend and all.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay asks why Millie didn't tell her about Tommy
jokes.punchline: Millie's explanation is accidentally devastating — she was 'protecting' Lindsay from jealousy, but the delivery is so guileless that it reads as an oblivious insult
jokes.explanation: The cringe here is perfect — Millie means absolutely no harm but has just delivered one of the most perfectly cutting things you can say to a teenage girl. The pause after '...since you don't have a boyfriend and all' is the comedy. Millie's innocence makes it hurt more than deliberate cruelty would.

jokes.text: 'Then how did he get them to name the band after him?' / 'I don't know, man. Maybe he's just a bad ass.' / 'If that's true, that's... amazing.' / 'Hey, maybe we should name our band Zarios.'
jokes.setup: They're discussing Santana — Nick points out the singer isn't Santana, Santana is the guitar player
jokes.punchline: The freaks work through the mystery of Santana's naming rights with genuine awe; the band-naming suggestion ('Zarios') is the non-sequitur button
jokes.explanation: The band discussion works because of the genuine reverence — 'maybe he's just a bad ass' treats naming a band as an almost supernatural feat. 'Zarios' as their proposed equivalent is a funny non-sequitur button that reveals they've missed the point entirely.

jokes.text: 'HEY, MAYBE WE SHOULD NAME OUR BAND ZARIOS.' (after the Santana discussion)
jokes.setup: The realization that Santana named his band after himself because he's a badass
jokes.punchline: Daniel immediately decides to apply this to their own band — naming it 'Zarios' (presumably after himself or someone in the group) with total confidence
jokes.explanation: 'Zarios' — the name itself is the joke. Whatever it means, it has the confident absurdity of a teenage band name arrived at through completely wrong logic. The callback to the Santana discussion makes it work as a comedic non-sequitur that is actually perfectly sequitur.

jokes.text: Harold's Scott Byron cautionary tale: kid who trick-or-treated into his 20s, now lives at home with his 90-year-old mother, never took a wife
jokes.setup: Harold argues Sam is too old to trick-or-treat
jokes.punchline: Harold has a highly specific anecdote that connects Halloween candy to adult failure to lifelong bachelorhood — the causal chain is absurd but delivered with complete conviction
jokes.explanation: Harold at his finest — the parental urban legend delivered with a straight face. The escalation from 'trick-or-treats too long' to 'never took a wife' is Harold's entire worldview compressed. 'Laughingstock of the community' and 'never took a wife' are Harold-specific formulations that couldn't come from any other character. Strong enough to nearly justify 8 — it's original, well-escalated, and deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: The Halloween candy-safety escalation: pins and razor blades → rat hair → heroin-injected candy by anti-Reagan hippies → poo dipped in chocolate in Fun-Sized wrappers
jokes.setup: Alan warns the geeks about dangerous Halloween candy
jokes.punchline: Each tier of candy threat is more absurd than the last — the political motivation (disrupting the election), the specificity of the chocolate-dipped poo, and 'I love Fun-Sized candy bars' as Bill's oblivious response
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best extended joke sequence outside the opening. The escalation is perfectly calibrated — each new danger tops the last. The anti-Reagan hippie motivation is historically sharp (it IS 1980). The chocolate-dipped poo detail is both disgusting and hilarious. Bill's 'I love Fun-Sized candy bars' as the innocent button to the grossest item is excellent character comedy. The structure and escalation earn this a high score.

jokes.text: Trick-or-treating logistics: 'BRING PILLOW CASES. NO GROCERY BAGS. IF YOUR BAG RIPS, YOU'RE ON YOUR OWN.'
jokes.setup: The geeks are planning their trick-or-treating strategy
jokes.punchline: Neal's extreme trick-or-treating operational planning — pillow cases are the professional choice; grocery bags are for amateurs — reveals these kids have thought deeply about candy collection efficiency
jokes.explanation: Neal's tactical approach to trick-or-treating ('if your bag rips, you're on your own') is funny because of its seriousness. These are kids applying adult operational discipline to a child's activity — which fits perfectly with the show's theme of the geeks being too young and too old simultaneously.

jokes.text: 'Actually, it sounds kinda fun. You mind if I come with you guys?' — Alan asking to join trick-or-treating after describing all the dangers
jokes.setup: Alan has just delivered an elaborate horror story about Halloween candy
jokes.punchline: Having terrified them, Alan immediately asks to join — revealing he wants to go trick-or-treating himself and the whole speech was just bullying cover
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback-subversion. Alan's entire speech was about why you shouldn't go trick-or-treating, and he immediately asks to join. This simultaneously reveals that he wants to go trick-or-treating (and is embarrassed about it) and that his whole performance was anxiety-based social theater. Good structural payoff.

jokes.text: 'Kinda hate when mom gets goofy like that.' — Sam's deadpan reaction to Jean dancing and singing Monster Mash
jokes.setup: Jean is enthusiastically singing Monster Mash in the kitchen
jokes.punchline: Sam's dry understatement — 'kinda hate' is doing a lot of work as a euphemism for deep adolescent mortification
jokes.explanation: Sam's delivery of adolescent embarrassment with maximum understatement is very consistent with his character. 'Kinda hate' as the measured equivalent of deep shame is economically perfect.

jokes.text: Lindsay discovers she's been given a Charlie Chaplin costume — which looks like Hitler: 'Lookin' for Chaplin... I'm only seeing Hitler.'
jokes.setup: Mom has laid out a costume for Lindsay — described as a Chaplin-esque look
jokes.punchline: The costume reads as Hitler rather than Chaplin; Lindsay's flat self-assessment is the punchline
jokes.explanation: The Hitler/Chaplin confusion is a genuine comedy dilemma — the visual codes of the two figures are identical (toothbrush mustache, small hat, dark outfit). Lindsay's deadpan assessment is excellent. The visual gag would be very strong on screen.

jokes.text: 'This is ridiculous. I look like Tom Selleck!' — Sam emerging in the Bionic Woman costume
jokes.setup: Mom has set out a costume — presumably the Bionic Woman — for Sam to wear
jokes.punchline: Sam in a Bionic Woman costume has no resemblance to Lindsay Wagner and every resemblance to Tom Selleck, apparently
jokes.explanation: The Tom Selleck comparison implies a mustache component to the Bionic Woman costume — clearly Jean's creation — and the gap between the intended glamour (Lindsay Wagner) and the achieved result (Sam looking like a mustachioed man) is a strong visual. 'Tom Selleck' is a very specific and funny comparator.

jokes.text: Jean playing the Bionic Woman on the phone: 'Hold on, I'm gonna put the phone on my bionic ear.' / 'Don't talk so loud! Don't forget, I've got bionic hearing.'
jokes.setup: Jean, in full Bionic Woman costume, is on the phone
jokes.punchline: Jean commits completely to the Bionic Woman bit while on a real phone call — treating the costume as conferring actual superpowers
jokes.explanation: Jean's complete unselfconscious immersion in the Bionic Woman persona while conducting a normal phone call is wonderful character comedy. The fact that she's using bionic hearing to ask someone not to speak loudly is perfectly absurd. Jean's earnest joy makes this work.

jokes.text: 'Hi, Mom. OK. You can... use the phone.' — Lindsay entering to find her mother mid-bionic-woman-phone-call
jokes.setup: Jean is deep into a bionic-character phone call in full costume
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's flat, resigned permission for her mother to continue using the phone she's already using is perfect embarrassed-teen deadpan — she has walked in on something she cannot process
jokes.explanation: 'You can... use the phone' — the permission Lindsay grants her own mother to continue an activity she has no power to stop is wonderfully calibrated teen humiliation. The pause before 'use the phone' implies she's computed several possible responses and settled on the safest one. Great economy.

jokes.text: 'Nice costume. You look like Richard Benjamin in Westworld.'
jokes.setup: One of Sam's friends arrives in a robot costume
jokes.punchline: Harold's compliment-as-film-reference reveals his specific cultural frame of reference — and that he knows his 1973 sci-fi Westerns
jokes.explanation: Harold's movie reference works both as a character beat (this is how Harold communicates) and as period humor — Westworld is an exactly Harold-appropriate reference. The specificity of 'Richard Benjamin' rather than just 'the human guy in Westworld' is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Harold to Bill in the Bionic Woman costume: 'What in the hell are you supposed to be?' / 'I'm a guy with a knife through his head.' / 'Well... Mission accomplished.'
jokes.setup: Harold sees the kid with a fake knife through his head and asks what he is
jokes.punchline: The kid's deadpan description of his own costume, and Harold's 'Mission accomplished' — a rare Harold compliment
jokes.explanation: 'Mission accomplished' is Harold at his dryest — a crisp, spare evaluation that works as both compliment and observation. The literalism of 'a guy with a knife through his head' from the kid is a good setup.

jokes.text: 'Wow, Harris, that looks really real.' / 'How do you know it isn't?'
jokes.setup: Jean admires Harris's fake-knife-through-head costume
jokes.punchline: Harris's deadpan threat/joke — delivered with complete ambiguity — creates a genuinely unsettling beat that's either very dark or completely silly
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — four words create genuine comedic ambiguity. Jean's reaction ('Oh!') tells us it landed. Harris's ability to make a simple question into something vaguely menacing is excellent deadpan. This is the type of line that comedy writers wish they'd written.

jokes.text: 'If I held you any closer, I'd be in back of ya.' — Harold trying to flirt with Jean in costume
jokes.setup: Harold and Jean are in their costumes together
jokes.punchline: Harold's old-fashioned flirting line — the kind of thing he probably thinks is smooth — is slightly off in a way that's very Harold
jokes.explanation: The line is a bit clunky, which is the joke — Harold's attempt at sophistication landing awkwardly. Character-appropriate but not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: Bill, in Bionic Woman costume: 'You know, Bill, you look really lovely.' / 'I'm Jaime Sommers, the Bionic Woman.' / 'Oh, of course you are!'
jokes.setup: Bill arrives in his Bionic Woman costume
jokes.punchline: Jean's enthusiastic 'Oh, of course you are!' — her genuine delight at the costume choice, not even pausing on the fact that a boy is dressed as the Bionic Woman
jokes.explanation: Good Jean moment. Her complete, uncomplicated acceptance of a boy in a Bionic Woman costume — 'Oh, of course you are!' — is both sweet and funny. No judgment, just delight. Character-specific and warm.

jokes.text: Bill in the Bionic Woman costume being complimented by Jean: 'You know, Bill, you look really lovely.' / 'I'm Jaime Sommers, the Bionic Woman.'
jokes.setup: Bill arrives at the door in the Bionic Woman costume
jokes.punchline: Jean's earnest compliment to a teenage boy in a woman's costume; Bill's solemn clarification of his character
jokes.explanation: Jean's genuine compliment is perfect — she means 'lovely' as a Halloween costume compliment and has no idea how it lands. Bill's solemn correction ('I'm Jaime Sommers') shows he's fully committed to the character. The juxtaposition of Jean's maternal sweetness and this surreal situation is very Freaks and Geeks.

jokes.text: Neal's mom staring; Sam says people are staring; Bill: 'Why is everybody staring at me? You'd think they'd never seen the Bionic Woman before.' / 'They just probably didn't know she was so hot in real life.'
jokes.setup: People keep staring at Bill in the Bionic Woman costume
jokes.punchline: Bill's obliviousness to why he's being stared at; Sam's deadpan explanation that Bill-as-Bionic-Woman is just very attractive
jokes.explanation: Sam's sincere-seeming explanation that Bill just happens to be a very attractive Bionic Woman is brilliant deadpan. Bill's complete acceptance of this is even better. The exchange is perfectly character-specific — only Sam would say this, only Bill would accept it.

jokes.text: 'I saw the real Jaime Sommers at the auto show last year. Her boobs weren't that big.'
jokes.setup: Bill is being admired as the Bionic Woman
jokes.punchline: Sam's clarification — he's met Lindsay Wagner and can assess how Bill compares — is delivered as completely matter-of-fact information
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. Sam's casual delivery of this information — he met the real Bionic Woman at an auto show, he has comparative data — is magnificently absurd. The specificity of 'auto show' grounds the absurdity perfectly. Sam is 14 and has strong opinions about Jaime Sommers' chest measurements in relation to Bill's padding. Highly quotable, excellently structured.

jokes.text: The trick-or-treat candy debate: rich people give the cheapest candy because 'it's how they stay rich'; Bill is allergic to peanuts, licorice, AND nougat; 'How can you possibly be allergic to nougat?'
jokes.setup: The boys debate candy strategy on Halloween night
jokes.punchline: Two separate jokes: Neal's class-warfare candy theory (rich people hoard); Bill's impossible nougat allergy (nougat is not a known allergen)
jokes.explanation: Two strong jokes in one beat. 'It's how they stay rich' is a fun class-politics joke from a 14-year-old. The nougat allergy is perfect Bill — his body is apparently allergic to more things than modern medicine can explain. 'How can you possibly be allergic to nougat?' is the right response and lands well. Strong character integration for both boys.

jokes.text: The full candy preference argument: wax lips / Pixie Sticks / Chunkies / candy corn — 'I must admit, I'm a wax lips man myself.'
jokes.setup: Candy preferences are declared with maximum seriousness
jokes.punchline: The adult-sounding 'I must admit, I'm a wax lips man myself' as the conclusion to a debate between children is the funniest item; each choice characterizes the speaker
jokes.explanation: 'I'm a wax lips man myself' is excellent because it applies wine-connoisseur syntax to wax lips, and the 'I must admit' is an admission of something slightly embarrassing. The candy debate as a character-revealing exercise is one of the show's strengths.

jokes.text: Harold in the vampire costume jumping out and shouting 'AAAAH!' at trick-or-treaters, making children cry; 'I'm not really a vampire. I own a sporting goods store.'
jokes.setup: Harold answers the door in his vampire costume to give candy
jokes.punchline: Harold terrifies children by accident; his reassurance ('I own a sporting goods store') is both completely non-soothing and extremely Harold
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best Harold moments. 'I'm not really a vampire. I own a sporting goods store.' is perfect — the parenthetical alternative identity he offers is completely useless as reassurance for terrified children. Why would owning a sporting goods store make a vampire less frightening? Harold thinks this is helpful. The specificity of 'sporting goods store' is what makes it exceptional — any other business would be slightly less funny. Strong enough to nearly hit 8 on craft.

jokes.text: The father refusing the homemade cookies: 'Those cookies could have razor blades or pins in them.' / 'I wouldn't do that.' — Jean's wounded response
jokes.setup: Jean offers her homemade Halloween cookies to children; the father refuses
jokes.punchline: The dangerous-candy warnings from earlier pay off here — Jean's genuine sweetness collides with rational parental paranoia; 'I wouldn't do that' as her defense is both sad and funny
jokes.explanation: The callback to the dangerous-candy paranoia lands well. Jean's total inability to understand why anyone would think she'd do this is genuinely funny and slightly heartbreaking — a mixture this show does especially well. 'I wouldn't do that' is so sincere it works as both comedy and character revelation.

jokes.text: Harold to Jean in cowboy/cowgirl costumes: 'Come on, Cowgirl. Let's head out to the barn and I'll give you a real good roll in the hay.'
jokes.setup: Harold and Jean are in their Halloween costumes after a rough evening
jokes.punchline: Harold's attempt at sexy Western role-play, delivered at the worst possible moment, in a hallway, with kids outside
jokes.explanation: Harold's romantic opportunism in the middle of Halloween chaos is consistent character comedy. The Western metaphors are very Harold. Jean's 'Not now, Harold' is the right response. Functional cringe.

jokes.text: Daniel's take on Halloween hypocrisy: religious town hangs pictures of devils and witches for Halloween; followed by a long burp as his philosophical conclusion
jokes.setup: Daniel is doing stoner-philosophy about community religious hypocrisy
jokes.punchline: The burp as the full stop on his argument — and Ken's 'That's lovely, you know. Your years in charm school really paid off.'
jokes.explanation: The burp-as-commentary structure is well-executed — Daniel nearly makes a genuine point before undermining it completely. Ken's 'charm school' response is a good comeback. The sequence characterizes both of them.

jokes.text: Ken: 'By the way, how are things going at ass school?' / 'Getting all As.'
jokes.setup: Ken mocks someone's institution after the charm school comment
jokes.punchline: The 'ass school / all As' pun — setting up a clear groaner but the response is immediate and well-timed
jokes.explanation: The pun is fairly obvious once 'ass school' is established, but the speed and confidence of 'Getting all As' makes it land better than it deserves. Functional.

jokes.text: The old man at the door: 'Aren't you guys a little old for this?' / 'We're only freshmen in high school.' / 'High school? That's too old.' / 'We're young at heart.'
jokes.setup: The geeks ring a doorbell for trick-or-treat
jokes.punchline: Sam and Neal try to negotiate down their age as a mitigating factor; the 'young at heart' defense is desperate and perfect
jokes.explanation: 'We're young at heart' from a 14-year-old trying to justify trick-or-treating is both pathetic and charming. The negotiation over whether 'freshmen' is a mitigating factor is true to the characters.

jokes.text: 'You look like the fricking Tin Man.' — the old man rejecting Sam's Gort costume identification
jokes.setup: Sam explains his Gort costume from The Day the Earth Stood Still
jokes.punchline: The old man doesn't know or care about the reference and reduces it to the more famous silver robot
jokes.explanation: Sam's obscure sci-fi reference being flattened to the most famous silver robot is a good nerd-humiliation beat. The dismissal is complete and unapologetic.

jokes.text: Bill at the door: 'Um, ma'am... I hope there aren't any peanuts in these peanuts. 'Cause if there are, I could d-die.'
jokes.setup: They receive circus peanuts
jokes.punchline: Bill's allergy declaration — 'I hope there aren't any peanuts in these peanuts' — is a beautifully recursive sentence; the 'd-die' stutter adds the right beat of genuine fear
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best individual joke. 'I hope there aren't any peanuts in these peanuts' is a legitimately great construction — the tautology is unavoidable but Bill speaks it with complete earnestness, as if there's a world where circus peanuts might not contain peanuts. The stutter on 'd-die' is the perfect button — he genuinely might. It's a callback to the earlier allergy conversation, it's maximally economical, it could only be Bill, and 'I could d-die' has become one of those lines that people who know the show repeat. This is genuinely exceptional television writing.

jokes.text: Parents dumping Jean's cookies on her lawn, all covered with ants: 'You should see all the parents who are dumping the cookies on your lawn. They're all covered with ants.'
jokes.setup: Jean's cookies have been refused by parents
jokes.punchline: The cookies are being physically ejected from the neighborhood and are attracting insects — the humiliation has a biological component now
jokes.explanation: The escalation of cookie-rejection from polite refusal to visible piles of ant-covered cookies is a strong image. The casual delivery of devastating news ('Happy Halloween!') is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Harold told to buy candy at Farmer Jack: 'What? Dressed like this?' — Harold in full vampire costume
jokes.setup: Jean asks Harold to go buy candy from the store since theirs isn't working
jokes.punchline: Harold's vanity — the vampire costume is apparently more embarrassing than anything else that's happened tonight
jokes.explanation: Harold's sense of dignity emerging at the worst moment is perfect. He terrified children, failed at cookie distribution, and now has costume-related dignity concerns about a grocery run. The visual of Harold in a vampire cape at Farmer Jack is implied and excellent.

jokes.text: 'Stupid kids' holiday.' — Harold's parting shot
jokes.setup: Harold reluctantly leaves in his vampire costume to buy candy
jokes.punchline: His summary verdict on Halloween, delivered as he waddles out in a cape
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — three words that encapsulate Harold's entire evening and worldview. The comedy comes from him wearing a cape while saying it.

jokes.text: 'YES, CONGRATULATIONS TO US ALL, INDEED.' (Ken, after they presumably egg or prank someone's property)
jokes.setup: The freaks have just done something they probably shouldn't have (based on context, egging a house)
jokes.punchline: Ken's outrageously formal 'Congratulations to us all, indeed' — Victorian phrasing applied to a delinquent act
jokes.explanation: Ken's deadpan formality ('Congratulations to us all, indeed') applied to minor vandalism is sharply funny. The aristocratic register contrasted with the delinquent act is a good Ken Miller joke. Quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: 'I'm so happy I'm not at the Ted Nugent concert.'
jokes.setup: The freaks have just done something mildly destructive on Halloween night
jokes.punchline: Ken's satisfaction is expressed entirely in terms of a concert he isn't attending — implying Ted Nugent is so terrible that literally any alternative is preferable
jokes.explanation: Ken's happiness being defined by a negative — the avoided Ted Nugent concert — is a wonderfully eccentric expression of contentment. The line implies a whole backstory (someone wanted him to go, he refused) while also being a perfect-register Ken observation. Strong character integration. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Harris receives a fun-sized candy bar from an adult: 'Oh, look. It's fun-sized.'
jokes.setup: Harris is egging with the freaks; an adult hands out candy
jokes.punchline: Harris's 'fun-sized' remark — a callback to the earlier chocolate-dipped-poo-in-fun-sized-wrappers warning — adds a layer of dark ambiguity
jokes.explanation: The callback to the fun-sized poo warning is understated — Harris says it with complete deadpan and the audience either makes the connection or doesn't. The economy is perfect.

jokes.text: 'I can't believe the hot-dog-on-a-stick girls saw us.' / 'That's months of groundwork down the drain.' / 'Like they'd ever go out with you.' / 'Hey, shut up. They like me. They always give me free lemonade refills.'
jokes.setup: The geeks have just been caught trick-or-treating by girls from the mall
jokes.punchline: Neal's 'groundwork' implies a serious long-term romantic campaign; the evidence of this campaign is free lemonade refills; Sam's 'like they'd ever go out with you' is uncharacteristically brutal
jokes.explanation: Neal's romantic strategy being exposed as 'free lemonade refills over months' is wonderful. The specificity of 'hot-dog-on-a-stick' (a real mall franchise) grounds the absurdity perfectly. Sam's ruthless honesty and Neal's indignation are well-played.

jokes.text: Ken produces a carton of eggs from his jacket: 'Breakfast, anyone?'
jokes.setup: Alan and his gang have cornered the geeks and are demanding their candy
jokes.punchline: Ken produces eggs from within his jacket at the exact moment they're needed, delivered with perfect nonchalance
jokes.explanation: This is excellent character comedy. 'Breakfast, anyone?' is the perfect line — it names the eggs with complete innocence while the threat is completely clear. Ken has been carrying eggs inside his jacket this whole time. The casual delivery is what makes it exceptional. High craft score justified: it's original, beautifully structured (the visual reveal plus the line), deeply character-specific (Ken would absolutely do this), and maximally economical. One of the episode's strongest moments.

jokes.text: 'Forget it. We went through a lot for this.' / 'Shut your mouth, you little girl.' / 'I'm not a little girl. I'm a Bionic Woman.' / 'Yeah? Prove it.'
jokes.setup: Alan demands Bill's candy; Bill refuses
jokes.punchline: Alan's 'little girl' insult prompts Bill to invoke his costume identity as a legitimate defense of his honor; Alan's 'Prove it' is perfect — a sincere challenge to demonstrate Bionic Woman capability
jokes.explanation: This is the best single exchange in the episode. Bill's 'I'm not a little girl. I'm a Bionic Woman' is a line that works on every level: it's character-perfect (Bill has been Jaime Sommers all night), it's logically consistent within the world of the joke, it's a genuine defense of dignity that accidentally makes him more vulnerable, and it escalates the absurdity without breaking. Alan's 'Prove it' completes it — the bully takes the bait completely. The episode has spent 25 minutes establishing Bill's commitment to the Bionic Woman identity, and this is the payoff. Legitimately exceptional writing.

jokes.text: 'Fell on my knife.' — the fake-knife kid narrating his fall
jokes.setup: Harris falls during the confrontation with Alan's gang
jokes.punchline: His description of falling on his prop — 'fell on my knife' — is accurate but sounds alarming, and his complete deadpan removes any urgency
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum comedy. 'Fell on my knife' stated calmly in the middle of a tense confrontation is exactly the right Harris energy. The callback to his earlier ambiguous 'How do you know it isn't?' makes this land harder — we've been prepped to find his fake knife funny.

jokes.text: Lindsay realizes they egged her little brother: 'Oh my God. We just egged my little brother.'
jokes.setup: The freaks have just pelted a group of kids in costume with eggs
jokes.punchline: The gort costume in the group was Sam — Lindsay's own brother; the recognition lands as both comedy and genuine dramatic horror
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff of the episode's two parallel stories colliding is well-earned. The comedy is that Gort (who was explicitly noted to look like the Tin Man) didn't register as Sam because Lindsay was looking for 'kids in costumes' not her brother. The dramatic irony has been building since the two storylines diverged. Strong narrative construction.

jokes.text: 'Oh my God. They're coming back to finish us off. Run for it!' — Sam seeing the car returning
jokes.setup: The freaks' car returns to the scene of the egging
jokes.punchline: Sam thinks they're being ambushed for a second round; they flee in terror from his own sister
jokes.explanation: Functional misdirection joke. The geeks fleeing from Lindsay is a good image but the line itself is fairly generic panic-comedy.

jokes.text: 'Is it just me, or did he seem upset?'
jokes.setup: Sam has walked away from the car refusing to speak to Lindsay
jokes.punchline: Ken's completely sincere query about whether Sam seemed upset — after Sam has very obviously refused to get in the car and walked away in silence
jokes.explanation: This is vintage Ken. His genuine uncertainty about Sam's emotional state — in the most obvious situation possible — is perfect character comedy. It could be obliviousness or deflecting humor, and the ambiguity is part of what makes it work. 'Would you shut up, please?' from Lindsay confirms the line landed. High character integration score justified — this could only be Ken.

jokes.text: 'I told you she'd be a drag.' — Kim's final verdict on Lindsay after the disaster
jokes.setup: Lindsay is distraught about egging her brother; the evening is over
jokes.punchline: Kim's complete lack of empathy and instant validation of her original position — delivered as the last word on the situation
jokes.explanation: Kim's self-vindication is completely in character — she's right, technically, though not for the reason she thinks. The complete lack of empathy is not played as cruelty but as Kim's operating mode. Strongly character-integrated.

jokes.text: Sam says he was attacked by 'freaks'; Harold: 'Freaks? Like circus freaks?' / 'Jean, I don't think there's bearded ladies running around throwing eggs at kids.'
jokes.setup: Sam reports being attacked by 'freaks'
jokes.punchline: Jean's first interpretation is literal carnival freaks; Harold's correction — while technically right — assumes the only alternative is circus freaks
jokes.explanation: Jean and Harold's inability to parse teenage slang is a good parent-comedy beat. 'I don't think there's bearded ladies running around throwing eggs at kids' is Harold at his most literalist — he's ruled out circus freaks as if that's the real concern. Nicely observed.

jokes.text: 'And you are on thin ice, missy.' — Harold to Lindsay
jokes.setup: Lindsay has returned after abandoning her mother on Halloween
jokes.punchline: Harold's measured threat — 'thin ice, missy' — delivered after the foxhole line, from a man in (presumably still) a vampire costume
jokes.explanation: 'Thin ice, missy' is Harold's particular brand of formal-sounding sternness. The 'missy' is the right touch. Functional character comedy.

tmdb_overview: Sam convinces Bill and Neal to trick-or-treat on Halloween, but they end up embarrassed while Lindsay and her friends wreak havoc in the neighborhood.
